name: Shrinivasa
dates_notes: Post-Nimbārka by guru-paramparā; dated by manuscript chronology.
core_thesis:
Śrīnivāsa is the architect rather than the innovator of Nimbārka-line Vedānta. He preserves the *svābhāvika-bhedābheda* of the *Vedānta-Pārijāta-Saurabha* (Nimbārka's bhāṣya on [*Brahma-Sūtra* 2.3.42](cite://badarayana/brahma-sutra/2.3.42)) and the *Daśa-Ślokī* (vv. 1, 4) without doctrinal alteration: *bheda* and *abheda* are intrinsic and simultaneous (*Vedānta-Kaustubha* on [*Brahma-Sūtra* 2.3.42](cite://badarayana/brahma-sutra/2.3.42)); *cit*, *acit*, and *Īśvara* — Kṛṣṇa with Rādhā — are co-eternally real (*Vedānta-Kaustubha* on [*Brahma-Sūtra* 1.1.1](cite://badarayana/brahma-sutra/1.1.1); cf. *Daśa-Ślokī* vv. 4–5); the *jīva* is eternally *aṃśa* of Brahman (*Vedānta-Kaustubha* on [*Brahma-Sūtra* 2.3.43](cite://badarayana/brahma-sutra/2.3.43)); *bhakti* and *prapatti* are the operative path (*Vedānta-Kaustubha* on [*Brahma-Sūtra* 3.2.24](cite://badarayana/brahma-sutra/3.2.24)). The contribution lies elsewhere.

Nimbārka's bhāṣya is famously compressed (Bose, *Vedānta-Pārijāta-Saurabha*, 1940, intro.). It states the school's reading at each *adhikaraṇa* but rarely stages the rival positions or answers them step by step. Śrīnivāsa's *Vedānta-Kaustubha* supplies the missing dialectical apparatus: he reconstructs the Advaitic (Śaṅkara, *Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya* 1.1.1, 2.1.14) and Bhāskarīya ([Bhāskara, *Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya* 2.1.14](cite://bhaskara/brahma-sutra-bhasya/2.1.14)) *pūrvapakṣas* that the *Pārijāta-Saurabha* leaves implicit, fixes the school's *uttarapakṣa* in technical form (*Vedānta-Kaustubha* on the same loci), and elaborates the *aṃśa-aṃśin* relation in detail (*Vedānta-Kaustubha* on [*Brahma-Sūtra* 2.3.42–43](cite://badarayana/brahma-sutra/2.3.42-43)).

The consequence is that *svābhāvika-bhedābheda* becomes defensible as a developed Vedāntic position rather than as a sectarian slogan (Dasgupta, *History of Indian Philosophy*, vol. III, ch. on Nimbārka). The Sanaka-sampradāya accordingly treats Śrīnivāsa's reading as canonical, and the later Nimbārka commentators — Keśava-Kāśmīrī's *Vedānta-Kaustubha-Prabhā* (sixteenth century) and the lineage that follows — build on his scaffolding rather than going back behind it to Nimbārka's own text.

engaged_works.title: Vedanta-Kaustubha
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored sub-commentary on Nimbārka's Pārijāta-Saurabha.
engaged_works.summary: The *Vedānta-Kaustubha* is Śrīnivāsa's *vyākhyā* on Nimbārka's *Vedānta-Pārijāta-Saurabha* and the canonical sub-commentary of the Sanaka-sampradāya. Three tasks structure the work. First, it explicates the *svābhāvika* — intrinsic, unconditioned — character of *bheda* and *abheda* between Brahman, *cit*, and *acit* (*Vedānta-Kaustubha* on [*Brahma-Sūtra* 2.3.42](cite://badarayana/brahma-sutra/2.3.42)), demarcating it cleanly from Bhāskara's *aupādhika* construal ([Bhāskara, *Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya* on 2.1.14](cite://bhaskara/brahma-sutra-bhasya/2.1.14)). Second, it stages the *pūrvapakṣa* against *māyā-vāda* (Śaṅkara, *Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya* 2.1.14, the *vivarta-vāda*) and against *aupādhika-bhedābheda* ([Bhāskara, *Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya* on 2.1.14](cite://bhaskara/brahma-sutra-bhasya/2.1.14)) and supplies the *uttarapakṣa* that Nimbārka's compressed bhāṣya only gestures at (*Vedānta-Kaustubha* on [*Brahma-Sūtra* 2.1.14, 2.3.42](cite://badarayana/brahma-sutra/2.1.14, 2.3.42)). Third, it elaborates the *aṃśa-aṃśin* relation by which *jīvas* are eternally portion-and-whole of Brahman, without either merger or severance (*Vedānta-Kaustubha* on [*Brahma-Sūtra* 2.3.43](cite://badarayana/brahma-sutra/2.3.43), on *aṃśo nānāvyapadeśāt*). The work also fixes the school's Vaiṣṇava commitments at the bhāṣya level — Kṛṣṇa with Rādhā as *parabrahman* (*Vedānta-Kaustubha* on [*Brahma-Sūtra* 1.1.1](cite://badarayana/brahma-sutra/1.1.1), *maṅgala-ślokas*), *prapatti* and *bhakti* as the means of liberation ([*Vedānta-Kaustubha* on 3.2.24](cite://srinivasa/vedanta-kaustubha/3.2.24)) — and supplies the structural template that Keśava-Kāśmīrī's *Vedānta-Kaustubha-Prabhā* will sub-comment in turn. The *Kaustubha* is therefore the operative doctrinal text of the Nimbārka line and the standard reference for any technical engagement with the school (Dasgupta, *History of Indian Philosophy*, vol. III).

key_passages.sanskrit_iast:
śrīkṛṣṇapādayugalaṃ śirasā namāmi
yatrānvitaḥ śrutigaṇo na virodham eti //
key_passages.text_critical_note: The accessible on-disk corpus is Roma Bose's translation edition rather than a Sanskrit print. This hemistich was recovered from a public quotation of the work and normalized into IAST; the second hemistich was not used because the quoted transmission is less secure.

pada_analysis.gloss: the pair of Śrī Kṛṣṇa's feet

pada_analysis.morphology: sandhi form (yatra + anvitaḥ); indecl + nom sg masc ppp

karaka_structure.note: goal entered into negatively with na

key_passages.english_close: To the pair of Śrī Kṛṣṇa's feet, I bow with my head. With reference to which [pair], the host of śrutis does not enter into contradiction.
key_passages.why_this_passage: Even as an invocation, this is not ornamental filler: it states that Kṛṣṇa is the personal referent in whom the śruti corpus coheres. Because the local corpus preserves the work chiefly in translation, this recoverable opening line is one of the safest loci for Śrīnivāsa's theological center.
key_passages.source_edition: Vedānta-pārijāta-saurabha of Nimbārka and Vedānta-kaustubha of Śrīnivāsa, Vol. I, ed./trans. Roma Bose (Calcutta: Royal Asiatic Society of Bengal, 1940); locus and translation verified from the local archive PDF, Sanskrit wording recovered from a public quotation of the same work because the on-disk copy is translation-only.

key_passages.locus_long: Vedānta-Kaustubha, opening prose statement of purpose
key_passages.sanskrit_iast:
tadājñayā taduktavartmanā tadanugrahakāmena
tacchiṣyeṇa mayā mṛdumitapado vedāntakaustubhaḥ
tadbhāvārthaprakāśako viduṣām upakārāya viracyate //
key_passages.text_critical_note: Recovered from a public quotation of the work and normalized into IAST. The local corpus confirms the corresponding English translation at Brahma-Sūtra 1.1.1 but does not print the Sanskrit text.

pada_analysis.gloss: by the path taught by him

samasa_vigrahas.resolution: mṛdūni mitāni ca padāni yasya saḥ

karaka_structure.note: agentive instrument in the passive construction

key_passages.english_close: By his command, by the path taught by him, with desire for his favor. By me, his disciple, the Vedānta-Kaustubha — of gentle and measured wording — [is being composed]. It is the manifester of the intended meaning of that [Brahma-Sūtra]. It is being composed for the benefit of the learned.
key_passages.why_this_passage: Śrīnivāsa's distinctive historical role is architectural rather than radically revisionary, and this sentence says so in his own voice: the work exists to unfold Nimbārka's terse bhāṣya faithfully. That makes it a fair representative passage, not a merely famous one.
key_passages.source_edition: Vedānta-pārijāta-saurabha of Nimbārka and Vedānta-kaustubha of Śrīnivāsa, Vol. I, ed./trans. Roma Bose (Calcutta: Royal Asiatic Society of Bengal, 1940); locus and translation verified from the local archive PDF, Sanskrit wording recovered from a public quotation of the same work because the on-disk copy is translation-only.

key_passages.sanskrit_iast:
tattvaṃ tāvan trividham |
cid-acid-brahma-bhedāt |
svādhīna-parādhīnau brahmajīvātmānāv ity arthaḥ ||
key_passages.text_critical_note: The first clause and the dependent gloss circulate together in secondary quotations of Śrīnivāsa's opening discussion. Because the local PDF preserves only the English translation, the normalized Sanskrit is limited to these short recoverable lines.

pada_analysis.gloss: because of the distinction among sentient, insentient, and Brahman

samasa_vigrahas.resolution: cit ca acit ca brahma ca; teṣāṃ bhedāt

key_passages.english_close: Reality, to begin with, is threefold. [It is so] because of the distinction among cit, acit, and Brahman. That is to say: Brahman and the jīva-self stand respectively as svādhīna (independent) and parādhīna (dependent on the other).
key_passages.why_this_passage: This compact opening formulation states the load-bearing ontology of Śrīnivāsa's school before later polemics or sub-arguments proliferate: three real categories and an asymmetrical dependence relation. Given the translation-only state of the local corpus, this is a better representative choice than a more famous but less programmatic line.
key_passages.source_edition: Vedānta-pārijāta-saurabha of Nimbārka and Vedānta-kaustubha of Śrīnivāsa, Vol. I, ed./trans. Roma Bose (Calcutta: Royal Asiatic Society of Bengal, 1940); locus and translation verified from the local archive PDF, Sanskrit wording recovered from a public quotation of the same work because the on-disk copy is translation-only.

key_passages.locus_long: Vedānta-Kaustubha on Brahma-Sūtra 2.3.42, jīva as aṃśa of Paramātman
key_passages.sanskrit_iast:
nāyaṃ jīvaḥ śrīpuruṣottamād atyantabhinnaḥ,
nāpy atyantābhinnaḥ, kintu paramātmano 'ṃśaḥ |
aṃśo hy eṣa parasya iti śruteḥ |
aṃśo hi śaktirūpo grāhyaḥ, parāsya śaktiḥ,
jīvo 'lpaśaktir asvatantraḥ iti śruteḥ ||
key_passages.text_critical_note: This passage is quoted with secure locus on Brahma-Sūtra 2.3.42 in a public discussion reproducing Vedānta-Kaustubha. Punctuation and sandhi have been normalized in IAST; the local corpus confirms the corresponding English translation but not the Sanskrit text.

pada_analysis.morphology: sandhi form (na + ayam); indecl + nom sg masc pron
abl sg masc; final -t > -d by sandhi before following vowel
particle; sandhi form of hi before vowel

pada_analysis.gloss: of the form of a power

karaka_structure.note: genitival relation in the predicate aṃśaḥ
scriptural warrant for belonging-to the supreme

key_passages.english_close: This jīva is not absolutely different from Śrī Puruṣottama. Nor again is it absolutely non-different. Rather, it is an aṃśa (part) of the supreme self. For scripture says, 'this one is indeed a part of the supreme'. And an 'aṃśa' is to be understood as being of the form of a power (śakti). [Thus scripture says]: 'it is the power of the supreme'. And: 'the jīva is a limited power, not independent' — so says scripture.

preserved_terms.first_use_gloss: (roughly: part, here a dependent power-expression rather than a severed fragment)

key_passages.why_this_passage: This is the cleanest recoverable statement of Śrīnivāsa's mature bhedābheda: the jīva is neither sheer otherness nor sheer identity, but a dependent power-as-part of the supreme. It fairly represents his position because it occurs exactly where he explains the Brahma-Sūtra on aṃśatva, not in a later caricature of an opponent.
key_passages.source_edition: Vedānta-pārijāta-saurabha of Nimbārka and Vedānta-kaustubha of Śrīnivāsa, Vol. I, ed./trans. Roma Bose (Calcutta: Royal Asiatic Society of Bengal, 1940); locus and translation verified from the local archive PDF, Sanskrit wording recovered from a public quotation of the same work because the on-disk copy is translation-only.
